title: Rust Tauri vs Electron for a Kanban Board desktop application
executiveSummary: Recommend Electron as the default choice for this Kanban board unless specific conditions hold, because the discussion's headline risk against Tauri (the classic WebKitGTK HTML5 drag-image bug) was shown to be moot — dnd-kit uses setPointerCapture + transforms rather than HTML5 drag (r1-msg-005), collapsing the central fault line. With the drag bug gone, both experts settled near a coin-flip (Electron 0.58, Tauri ~0.54), meaning the decision is driven by context inputs, not framework superiority. Electron wins when any of these hold: Linux must be first-class across many distros (WebKitGTK version skew is genuinely unbounded — r1-msg-008), the team lacks Rust fluency and launch speed matters, you lean on Chromium-uniform ecosystem edges (data-grid, offline-sync, charting — r1-msg-005), or projected usage stays below the ~50k-install / 100k-250k always-open-DAU crossover where per-user RAM and Chromium-0-day re-bundle SLA costs start to dominate (r1-msg-004). Tauri wins when the inverse holds: a small/controlled Linux target (or Win/mac-only), Rust fluency already on the team, a footprint hard-requirement (<10MB installer / low idle RAM), high always-open concurrency above the crossover, or a supply-chain ban on bundled Chromium (r1-msg-002, r1-msg-004). The decision is reversible cheaply only before the long-tail probe: gate Tauri on a broadened 5-eng-day spike covering DnD AND the non-DnD long tail (IME/CJK, file dialogs, print/PDF, codecs, distro skew) per r1-msg-008. Critically, both experts conceded the shell may be lower-stakes than the sync/collaboration moat (r1-msg-006, r1-msg-007), so do not over-invest the decision — pick Electron to remove a launch-risk dependency and spend the saved engineering weeks on the differentiator unless a footprint/Linux/scale gate flips it to Tauri.

insights.title: The headline WebKitGTK drag bug is moot because dnd-kit avoids HTML5 drag
insights.description: The framed central fault line — whether WebKitGTK degrades multi-column drag-and-drop enough to erase Tauri's footprint savings — dissolved when it was established that dnd-kit does not use the HTML5 drag-and-drop API; it uses setPointerCapture + CSS transforms, so the classic WebKitGTK drag-image rendering bug never fires. This converts DnD from a potential disqualifier into a bounded perf/polish question testable by a concrete 5-engineer-day spike.
insights.confidenceReason: Resolved by argument, not majority: a specific technical mechanism was named, went unrefuted, and both experts accepted it.

supportingEvidence.summary: dnd-kit avoids HTML5 drag so the WebKitGTK drag-image bug is moot; residual risk is perf/polish gated on a 5-eng-day spike.
velocity-lead's strongest remaining DnD claim narrowed to accessible KeyboardSensor + live ARIA — polish, not the rendering bug.
moderator framed drag-degradation as the primary fault line, which the spike + dnd-kit mechanism defused.

dissentingViews.summary: accessible keyboard DnD + live ARIA on WebKitGTK remains an untested polish risk.

insights.title: The long tail splits into bounded (Tauri v2 plugins/OS APIs) and unbounded (Linux engine variance)
insights.description: The recurring non-DnD tax is not monolithic. BOUNDED and largely solved by stable Tauri v2 plugins/native OS APIs: file dialogs, notifications, updater, signing/notarization, and IME/CJK via OS-native webview (a Tauri strength). Genuinely UNBOUNDED: WebKitGTK version skew across distros, print/PDF fidelity, codec/media via system GStreamer. The unbounded risks concentrate almost entirely on Linux.
insights.confidenceReason: Resolved by argument with explicit concessions; runtime-architect adopted it while shifting his own confidence down (conceding against interest).

supportingEvidence.summary: runtime-architect split the long tail bounded vs unbounded and proposed broadening the spike.
contrarian surfaced the non-DnD long tail + hidden Rust plumbing as the real recurring tax.

insights.title: Electron pays engine-divergence once; WebKitGTK is the engine that varies across distros
insights.description: Because Electron bundles one Chromium across all platforms, it pays per-engine divergence cost ONCE; WebKitGTK is precisely the engine whose behavior varies across distros, so the unbounded part of the long tail is structurally Electron's advantage. Complementary to the bounded/unbounded split — together they localize residual risk to Linux-first-class scenarios.
insights.confidenceReason: Structural argument (bundled-engine uniformity vs system-engine variance), effectively conceded by runtime-architect classifying distro skew as UNBOUNDED.

supportingEvidence.summary: the long-tail critique FAVORS Chromium uniformity — Electron pays divergence once; WebKitGTK varies.
runtime-architect implicitly conceded by placing distro skew in the UNBOUNDED column.

dissentingViews.summary: IME/CJK via OS-native webview is a Tauri strength, so engine uniformity is not a blanket win.

insights.title: Footprint cost is real but mostly aggregate RAM, not per-user bandwidth
insights.description: velocity-lead self-corrected: Electron delta-updates make download bandwidth negligible (~1.5 eng-weeks/yr maintenance, not 150MB/user/update). The genuine conceded cost is per-user idle RAM (Electron ~250-400MB vs Tauri ~80-150MB) aggregated across an always-open tool. This sets the crossover thresholds and prevents footprint being either dismissed or overstated.
insights.confidenceReason: Self-correction against interest by the Electron advocate, consistent with runtime-architect's numbers.

supportingEvidence.summary: delta-updates make bandwidth negligible; conceded per-user RAM is a real aggregate cost.
footprint baseline: Tauri 5-15MB / 80-150MB RAM vs Electron 80-150MB / 250-400MB RAM.

insights.title: Concrete decision gates and crossover thresholds make the choice non-mushy
insights.description: Quantified deciders: maintenance-vs-velocity crossover ~100k-250k always-open DAU on low-RAM, OR ~50k installs at the moment a Chromium 0-day forces an emergency re-bundle under SLA. Below these, Electron's delivery-speed advantage dominates; above, Tauri's footprint/attack-surface pays back. Plus binary gates (Linux first-class?, Rust fluency?, footprint hard-req?, Chromium ban?).
insights.confidenceReason: Thresholds are one expert's order-of-magnitude estimate, directionally agreed but not independently validated.

supportingEvidence.summary: crossover ~100k-250k always-open DAU, or ~50k installs under a Chromium 0-day SLA.
flip conditions: hard <10MB/low-RAM, existing Rust fluency, Chromium ban, or spike parity.
flip conditions: WebKitGTK breaks board, ecosystem lib gap, or ship-pressure favors one engine.

insights.title: The shell choice may be lower-stakes than the sync/collaboration moat
insights.description: Both experts at 0.54-0.60 on opposite picks is itself signal: the desktop shell may be low-stakes relative to the product differentiator (sync/collaboration, out of scope). velocity-lead conceded lower stakes but argued the implication still favors Electron — remove a launch-risk dependency and spend saved weeks on the moat. Reframes the question to 'which framework frees the most capacity for what matters'.
insights.confidenceReason: Resolved by argument and conceded, but rests on a scope-adjacent premise (the sync moat is excluded here).

supportingEvidence.summary: near-tie implies the shell is low-stakes vs the sync differentiator.
conceded lower stakes but argued they still favor Electron.

agreements: The classic WebKitGTK HTML5 drag-image bug is moot because dnd-kit uses setPointerCapture + transforms, not HTML5 drag (r1-msg-005).
A concrete WebKitGTK spike is the right gate; broaden it beyond DnD to a long-tail probe pass (r1-msg-005, r1-msg-008).
Per-user idle RAM is a real aggregate cost; download bandwidth is not, thanks to delta-updates (r1-msg-004).
Electron's Chromium uniformity genuinely saves time on ecosystem edges — data-grid, offline-sync, charting (r1-msg-005).
Tauri v2 plugins + native OS APIs bound a large part of the long tail (r1-msg-008).
The shell choice is lower-stakes than team Rust-readiness and the sync/collaboration moat (r1-msg-006, r1-msg-007).
Both converged to a near coin-flip (Electron 0.58, Tauri ~0.54), so context inputs decide.

minorityReport.position: Accessible keyboard DnD (dnd-kit KeyboardSensor) + live ARIA announcements cannot be cheaply matched on WebKitGTK.
minorityReport.reason: The one DnD-adjacent claim surviving 'drag bug is moot' — about screen-reader/keyboard polish, never tested.
minorityReport.note: Unrefuted; it is one of the 4 numeric gates in the proposed spike. Treat as open until the spike runs; not a disqualifier.

minorityReport.position: IME/CJK input via OS-native webview is a Tauri STRENGTH, partially offsetting Electron's engine-uniformity advantage.
minorityReport.reason: The native webview inherits the OS's mature IME, which can beat bundled Chromium for CJK.
minorityReport.note: Un-refuted; qualifies (does not overturn) the 'pays divergence once' counter.

minorityReport.position: A Linux-only split build (Tauri on Win/mac + Electron/CEF on Linux) captures footprint wins where Linux is safe.
minorityReport.reason: Targets the localized residual risk (Linux distro skew) rather than conceding the platform.
minorityReport.note: Proposed concession path; carries a real cost of two shells / two code paths.

unresolvedDebates.topic: Does the non-DnD long tail net out bounded-and-cheap or as a recurring tax that erases Tauri's footprint savings?
positions: runtime-architect: largely bounded; only distro skew + print/PDF + codecs are genuinely unbounded (r1-msg-008).
velocity-lead: even the unbounded part is structurally Electron's win (r1-msg-007).
contrarian: unproven until shown bounded/cheap AND Rust plumbing one-time (r1-msg-006).
unresolvedDebates.whyUnresolved: No empirical data: the long-tail probe proposed in r1-msg-008 has not been run, so print/PDF, codec, and distro-skew costs are asserted, not measured.

unresolvedDebates.topic: Where exactly is the maintenance-vs-velocity crossover, and how hard is the Chromium-0-day SLA trigger?
positions: velocity-lead: ~100k-250k DAU or ~50k installs under SLA (r1-msg-004).
runtime-architect: footprint + attack surface justify Tauri earlier (r1-msg-002).
unresolvedDebates.whyUnresolved: A single expert's order-of-magnitude estimate, never validated; the SLA trigger depends on whether the product carries a security SLA — a context input.

journey: r1-msg-002: Tauri @0.60 on footprint + attack surface.
r1-msg-005: held @0.60 but GATED on a 5-eng-day spike; established the drag bug is moot; conceded Chromium uniformity saves time on ecosystem edges.
r1-msg-008: SHIFTED to ~0.54 (minor); conceded spike selection-bias + Rust plumbing; split the long tail and proposed broadening the spike.
r1-msg-001: Electron @0.60 on dnd-kit + React/TS reuse + launch in weeks.
r1-msg-004: softened to @0.58; self-corrected the bandwidth myth; conceded per-user RAM; narrowed DnD case to accessible keyboard + ARIA.
r1-msg-007: HELD @0.58, re-grounded from DnD-risk to engine-variance + team-readiness; conceded lower stakes than the sync moat.
keyTurningPoints: turning the contrarian's long-tail critique into a pro-Electron argument (r1-msg-006 -> r1-msg-007).

openQuestions.question: Does the broadened spike (DnD + long-tail probe) pass on the actual target distros (autoscroll fps, keyboard reorder + SR live-region, print/PDF, codec/media)?
openQuestions.whyOpen: It has not been run; only proposed (r1-msg-005, r1-msg-008). Most decision-relevant unknown — converts unbounded-Linux risk from assertion to measurement.
openQuestions.suggestedApproach: Run the 5-eng-day spike with 4 numeric DnD gates plus a long-tail probe across the 2-3 first-class distros; any unbounded-column failure is a flip-to-Electron (or Linux-split) signal.

openQuestions.question: Is Linux a first-class, broad-distro target, or controlled/narrow (or Win/mac-only)?
openQuestions.whyOpen: The only genuinely unbounded risk localizes to Linux, but the actual Linux requirement is a context input never fixed.
openQuestions.suggestedApproach: Make the requirement explicit. Broad-distro Linux -> Electron (or split). Narrow/no Linux -> the unbounded risk vanishes and Tauri becomes competitive.

openQuestions.question: What is the realistic always-open DAU / install trajectory over 12-24 months vs the ~50k-install / 100k-250k-DAU crossover?
openQuestions.whyOpen: Thresholds are defined (r1-msg-004) but the product's scale projection is out of scope.
openQuestions.suggestedApproach: Plot conservative and aggressive curves against the crossover; if the aggressive curve crosses ~100k always-open DAU AND a security SLA is likely, weight Tauri more.

openQuestions.question: Does the team have / can cheaply acquire Rust fluency, and is the Rust CI/signing/notarization plumbing genuinely one-time?
openQuestions.whyOpen: contrarian flagged Rust plumbing + hiring (r1-msg-006); runtime-architect conceded the cost but claimed plumbing is bounded via tauri-action (r1-msg-008) — neither validated.
openQuestions.suggestedApproach: Assess fluency honestly; cost the tauri-action setup as a one-time spike. No fluency + launch pressure -> Electron; existing fluency -> Tauri costs shrink.

recommendations.action: Default to Electron for the Kanban shell and redirect the saved engineering weeks to the sync/collaboration moat.
recommendations.risk: Per-user idle RAM aggregate cost past the crossover; a Chromium 0-day forcing emergency re-bundle under SLA. Both bounded and back-loaded.
recommendations.prerequisite: No footprint hard-requirement, no Chromium ban, projected always-open DAU below ~100k-250k within the horizon.

recommendations.action: Before committing either way, run the broadened spike (4 numeric DnD gates + non-DnD long-tail probe) on the intended first-class distros as the decision gate.
recommendations.risk: Spike selection bias — mitigated by explicitly including unbounded-column probes (print/PDF, codecs, distro skew).
recommendations.prerequisite: Define the first-class platform/distro list first.

recommendations.action: Choose Tauri if Linux is narrow/controlled (or Win/mac-only) AND any of: footprint hard-requirement, existing Rust fluency, scale above the crossover, or a Chromium ban — and the spike passes.
recommendations.risk: Unbounded Linux long tail (distro skew, print/PDF, GStreamer codecs) + accessible-keyboard-DnD polish remain until the spike proves them bounded.
recommendations.prerequisite: Spike passes all numeric gates and the long-tail probe shows no unbounded failures on target distros.

recommendations.action: Keep the Linux-only split build (Tauri Win/mac + Electron/CEF Linux) as a fallback if footprint matters on Win/mac but Linux variance fails the spike.
recommendations.risk: Two shells / two rendering+DnD code paths roughly doubles cross-platform QA surface.
recommendations.prerequisite: Win/mac footprint win is material AND the Linux spike fails specifically on the unbounded column.

metaObservations: The structure did its job: the moderator's fault-line framing (r1-msg-003) + a 4/10 disagreement budget pushed the experts to locate and dissolve the actual crux (the drag bug) rather than talk past each other, and the contrarian's stress test (r1-msg-006) prevented a premature 'the spike is a sufficient statistic' consensus — producing the useful bounded-vs-unbounded long-tail split. The most load-bearing moves were concessions against interest (velocity-lead's delta-update self-correction + RAM concession in r1-msg-004; runtime-architect's downshift to 0.54 in r1-msg-008), which is why confidence tracks argument quality, not the near-even 0.58/0.54 vote. The near coin-flip is a real finding, not indecision: once the drag bug fell, framework superiority stopped being the variable and context inputs (Linux scope, scale, Rust-readiness, the out-of-scope sync moat) became the true deciders. The one structural gap: those decisive inputs were ruled out of scope by the problem definition, so the discussion correctly identified the answer's shape but could not close it without inputs it was forbidden to use.
